body_txt: In Japan and Japanese media, there exists a subculture known as ''Gyaru'' , or "Gal" culture. This subculture often involves the exaggerated emulation and use of popular Western fashion trends, tanned skin, dyed hair, and excessive use of makeup. Gyaru culture is split into three subcultures: Kogal, Ganguro, and Yanmanba. Gyaru adherents are almost Always Female , but there do exist rare male variants, who are referred to as Gyaru-o . Initially created from the rebellious attitudes embraced by Japanese Delinquents , the cultures originated in the 1970s, and gained popularity in the late 1990s and early 2000s. Far from being an underground thing, the Gyaru style actually has a huge influence on Japanese fashion trends, even today. The relationship between female Japanese delinquents and Gyaru is complex. Not all delinquents are Gyaru nor all Gyaru are delinquents, especially in recent decades, where both things have followed more divergent roads, but there is still some overlap between them, especially given that Gyaru subculture is certainly a form of rebellion against traditional Japanese social rules. Compensated Dating and other barely legal ways to earn income for their lifestyle are the kind of crimes common Gyaru are most likely to commit.    Gyaru Girls will often have their fashion style pointed out in the narrative. Compare The Fashionista , and compare and contrast with the Western equivalent Valley Girl , which gyaru are often given the speech patterns of in English dubs. See also Nonconformist Dyed Hair , for examples outside the gyaru subculture/s that dye hair to subvert societal norms. Gyaru subculture developed in response to, and is therefore to some extent a deliberate inversion of, the more traditional feminine ideal of the Yamato Nadeshiko .
